explanation: 'Tomorrow I'll speak to the director': Domani parlerò con il direttore. When you say what you will do tomorrow, you reach for the future. A regular -are verb builds its future stem by shifting the theme vowel a to e: parlare gives parler-, then the io ending -ò: parlerò. Keep the a (parlarò) and you have made the commonest -are slip. The accent on the -ò is obligatory.
examiner_note: The a-to-e shift is the load-bearing skill; parlarò is the diagnostic miss. Pairs with the dare/stare/fare items, which deliberately do NOT shift.

explanation: 'Next year Marco will work in the city centre': L'anno prossimo Marco lavorerà in centro. Marco is he, so this is the third person singular. A regular -are verb shifts its theme vowel a to e and adds -à: lavorare gives lavorerà. The accent on the final -à is obligatory and distinguishes the future (lavorerà, he will work) from forms without it.

'Where will you live after you graduate?': Tu dove abiterai dopo la laurea? Tu is right there in the sentence, so you want the second person singular. The -are theme vowel a shifts to e and the tu ending -ai joins on: abitare gives abiterai. Leaving the a (abitarai) is the typical first-conjugation miss.

'The guests will arrive at eight': Gli ospiti arriveranno alle otto. Gli ospiti is they, so this is the third person plural. The -are theme vowel shifts a to e and the loro ending -anno joins on, with its double n: arrivare gives arriveranno. Watch two slips at once here: keeping the a (arrivaranno) and writing a single n (arriverano).

'Next year we'll study Italian together': L'anno prossimo studieremo l'italiano insieme. Studiare is a plain -iare verb (not -ciare or -giare), so the i belongs to the stem and stays right through the future: studi- shifts the theme vowel a to e and adds the noi ending -emo, giving studieremo. Drop that i (studeremo) and you have removed a stem letter; keep the a (studiaremo) and you have missed the shift.
examiner_note: Plain -iare keeps its i; the studerò-style drop is the guard. No dedicated leaf for plain -iare, so this lives under regular_are per the dispatch.

explanation: 'What time will you have dinner this evening?': Voi a che ora cenerete stasera? Voi is in the sentence, so you want the second person plural. The -are theme vowel shifts a to e and the voi ending -ete joins on: cenare gives cenerete. The voi ending carries no written accent, unlike the 1sg and 3sg.

prompt: Completa: 'Domani ____ il treno delle nove.' (prendere, io)
explanation: 'Tomorrow I'll take the nine o'clock train': Domani prenderò il treno delle nove. A regular -ere verb keeps its theme vowel e (no shift, unlike -are) and adds the io ending -ò: prendere gives prenderò. Don't be tempted to drop the vowel and syncopate (prendrò); that contraction belongs only to the closed irregular set like avere and vedere, not to a regular -ere verb.

'She'll write you a letter': Lei ti scriverà una lettera. Lei is the subject, so this is the third person singular. The -ere theme vowel stays and the ending -à joins on: scrivere gives scriverà. Scrivere is regular in the future, so resist syncopating it (scrivrà) by false analogy with avrò or vedrò. The accent on -à is obligatory.

prompt: Completa: 'I ragazzi ____ il libro in classe.' (leggere)
explanation: 'The boys will read the book in class': I ragazzi leggeranno il libro in classe. I ragazzi is they, so this is the third person plural. The -ere theme vowel stays and the loro ending -anno joins on, with its double n: leggere gives leggeranno. A single n (leggerano) is the spelling slip to avoid.

'Will you close the door when you go out?': Tu chiuderai la porta quando esci? Tu is supplied, so this is the second person singular. The regular -ere stem keeps its e and adds -ai: chiudere gives chiuderai. Chiudere is regular in the future, so keep the full stem rather than syncopating it (chiudrai).

'The flight will leave on time': Il volo partirà in orario. Il volo is the subject, so this is the third person singular. A regular -ire verb keeps its i and adds the ending -à: partire gives partirà. The -ire class makes no theme-vowel shift, so the i carries straight through. The accent on -à is obligatory.

'In time I'll understand everything': Con il tempo capirò tutto. Capire takes the -isc- insert in the present (capisco), but the future has no -isc- at all: the stem is simply capir-, and the io ending -ò joins straight on, giving capirò. Carrying the present's insert into the future (capiscirò) is the slip to avoid.
examiner_note: Suppressed: a 'Regular -ire' breadcrumb would tell the learner capire is regular in the future (no -isc-), which is the very thing under test. The diagnostic miss is capiscirò (the present's -isc- dragged in); capiro guards the dropped accent.

explanation: 'They'll finish the work tomorrow': Loro finiranno il lavoro domani. Finire is an -isc- verb in the present (finisco), but in the future the insert disappears entirely: the stem is finir- and the loro ending -anno joins on, giving finiranno. Two slips lurk here: dragging in the -isc- (finisciranno) and writing a single n (finirano).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the capire item: the breadcrumb would reveal that the future drops the present's -isc-.

explanation: 'You'll sleep well tonight': Tu dormirai bene stanotte. Tu is supplied, so this is the second person singular. Dormire is a plain -ire verb with no -isc- insert anywhere, so the future is simply the stem dormir- plus -ai: dormirai. The i of the -ire class carries straight through.

translation.reveal: cercare - to look for / to search

explanation: 'Tomorrow I'll look for a new job': Domani cercherò un nuovo lavoro. Cercare ends in -care, where the c sounds hard like a k. Because the future ending begins with the front vowel e (cerc- + er + ò), an h is slipped in to keep that hard sound: cercherò. Leave the h out (cercerò) and you would be reading it as 'cher-'. The h appears in every person of the future, not just one or two as in the present.
examiner_note: Suppressed: a '-care/-gare add h' breadcrumb names the rule-output (the inserted h) the item tests.

explanation: 'We'll be the ones paying the bill': Il conto lo pagheremo noi. Pagare ends in -gare, where the g is hard. The future ending begins with e (pag- + er + emo), so an h goes in to keep the g hard: pagheremo. Without the h (pageremo) the g would soften to a 'j' sound.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the cercare item.

explanation: 'The team will play at home on Sunday': La squadra giocherà in casa domenica. La squadra is the subject, so this is the third person singular. Giocare ends in -care, so an h is inserted before the future e to keep the c hard: giocherà. Two things to keep here: the h (without it, giocerà reads wrong) and the obligatory accent on -à.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other care_gare items. Both rejected forms (giocera, giocerà) are genuine no-h misses; the accent alone is orthography, left to the soft accent guard.

explanation: 'The teachers will explain the rule': I professori spiegheranno la regola. I professori is they, so this is the third person plural. Spiegare ends in -gare, so the h goes in before the future e to keep the g hard, and the loro ending -anno adds its double n: spiegheranno. Watch both the missing h (spiegeranno) and the single n (spiegherano).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other care_gare items.

explanation: 'On Monday I'll start the new course': Lunedì comincerò il nuovo corso. Cominciare ends in -ciare, where the i is there only to soften the c before a and o. In the future the ending already starts with e, which softens the c on its own, so the redundant i is dropped: cominc- + er + ò gives comincerò. Keep that i (comincierò) and you have doubled up a softener that is no longer needed.
examiner_note: Suppressed: a '-ciare/-giare drop the i' breadcrumb names the rule-output the item tests.

prompt: Completa: 'A Natale ____ troppo, come sempre.' (mangiare, loro)
explanation: 'At Christmas they'll eat too much, as always': A Natale mangeranno troppo, come sempre. Mangiare ends in -giare. The future ending starts with e, which already softens the g, so the redundant i is dropped: mang- + er + anno gives mangeranno. Two slips to watch: keeping the i (mangieranno) and writing a single n (mangerano).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the cominciare item.

explanation: 'Will you leave me your number?': Tu mi lascerai il tuo numero? Tu is supplied, so this is the second person singular. Lasciare ends in -sciare; the i softens the sc before a, but the future e does that job, so the i is dropped: lasc- + er + ai gives lascerai. Keeping the i (lascierai) is the typical miss.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other ciare_giare items.

explanation: 'Next summer we'll travel in Asia': L'estate prossima viaggeremo in Asia. Viaggiare ends in -giare, so the i that softened the g in the infinitive is dropped before the future e: viagg- + er + emo gives viaggeremo. Keep the i (viaggieremo) and you have left in a softener the e already provides.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other ciare_giare items.

explanation: 'Tomorrow I'll have more free time': Domani avrò più tempo libero. Avere is one of the syncopated verbs: the theme vowel drops out, leaving the root directly before -r- and the endings. So avere gives the stem avr-, then -ò: avrò. Keep the vowel (averò) and you have failed to syncopate. The accent on -ò is obligatory, and this very form, avrò, is also the auxiliary you will use to build the futuro anteriore.
examiner_note: Suppressed: a 'syncopated stems' breadcrumb tells the learner the verb drops its theme vowel, which is the whole skill. The endings are regular, so the stem is the entire test.

explanation: 'She'll go to Rome by train': Lei andrà a Roma in treno. Lei is the subject, so this is the third person singular. Andare syncopates: the theme vowel drops, giving the stem andr-, then the ending -à: andrà. Note that even though andare is an -are verb, there is no a-to-e shift to worry about here because the vowel is gone altogether. The accent on -à is obligatory.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the avere item.

prompt: Completa: 'Per arrivare in tempo, ____ partire presto.' (dovere, noi)
explanation: 'To arrive on time, we'll have to leave early': Per arrivare in tempo, dovremo partire presto. Dovere syncopates: the theme vowel drops, giving the stem dovr-, then the noi ending -emo: dovremo. Keep the vowel (doveremo) and you have a regular-looking form that Italian does not use for this verb.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other syncopated items.

translation.reveal: potere - to be able to / can

explanation: 'They won't be able to come to the party': Loro non potranno venire alla festa. Loro is the subject, so this is the third person plural. Potere syncopates to the stem potr-, then takes the loro ending -anno: potranno. Watch two things: the dropped vowel (not poteranno) and the double n (not potrano).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other syncopated items.

explanation: 'Tomorrow you'll know the result': Tu domani saprai il risultato. Tu is supplied, so this is the second person singular. Sapere syncopates to the stem sapr-, then takes -ai: saprai. Don't confuse this future stem with essere's sar-; sapere keeps its p (sapr-).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other syncopated items.

explanation: 'I'll see you tomorrow at the office': Ci vedrò domani in ufficio. Vedere is an -ere verb that syncopates in the future: the theme vowel drops, giving the stem vedr-, then -ò: vedrò. This is exactly why a regular -ere verb like prendere must NOT be syncopated (prenderò, never prendrò): the contraction is reserved for this closed irregular set. The accent on -ò is obligatory.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other syncopated items; cross-references the regular_ere items, where the same contraction is the miss.

explanation: 'When they're grown up they'll live abroad': Da grandi vivranno all'estero. Vivere syncopates to the stem vivr-, then takes the loro ending -anno: vivranno. Like vedere, it is an -ere verb that contracts, so keep the v doubled-up cluster vivr- and the loro double n.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other syncopated items. Lower-frequency than avere/andare, so set at B1.

explanation: 'Careful, if you run like that you'll fall': Attento, se corri così cadrai. Cadere syncopates to the stem cadr-, then takes the tu ending -ai: cadrai. The theme vowel drops just as it does in avrò and vedrò; keep it (caderai) and the verb looks regular, which it is not.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other syncopated items. Lower-frequency, so B1.

explanation: 'Tomorrow I'll want to go out with you': Domani vorrò uscire con voi. Volere is a double-r verb: when the theme vowel drops, the cluster that is left assimilates into a doubled r. So volere gives the stem vorr-, then -ò: vorrò. The signature miss is a single r (vorò); the doubled r is the whole point. The accent on -ò is obligatory.
examiner_note: Suppressed: an 'assimilated double-r stems' breadcrumb hands over the doubled r the item tests. Single-r vorò is the diagnostic miss and is in must_not_include.

explanation: 'She'll come to the party with us': Lei verrà alla festa con noi. Lei is the subject, so this is the third person singular. Venire is a double-r verb: the stem assimilates to verr-, then takes -à: verrà. A single r (verà) is the classic slip. Note also that venire is irregular here, so it does not build the regular -ire future (venirà). The accent on -à is obligatory.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the volere item.

explanation: 'Will you keep my seat for me?': Tu mi terrai il posto? Tu is supplied, so this is the second person singular. Tenere is a double-r verb: the stem assimilates to terr-, then takes -ai: terrai. The doubled r is the signature; a single r (terai) is the miss.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other double_r items.

explanation: 'This evening we'll stay at home': Stasera rimarremo a casa. Rimanere is a double-r verb: the stem assimilates to rimarr-, then takes the noi ending -emo: rimarremo. Note the a stays in the stem (rimarr-, not rimer-); only the vowel before the ending is lost. A single r (rimaremo) is the slip.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other double_r items. Slightly lower-frequency, so B1.

explanation: 'At the party they'll drink only water': Alla festa berranno solo acqua. Bere is a double-r verb in the future: the stem assimilates to berr-, then takes the loro ending -anno: berranno. The future is built on the short ber-, not the bev- stem bere uses in the present (bevo) and imperfect; this is a place those two stems diverge. Watch the single r (beranno) and the single n (berrano).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other double_r items. The bev-/berr- split is worth flagging; it parallels the present-tree note on bere's bev- expansion.

explanation: 'The children will want to know everything': I bambini vorranno sapere tutto. I bambini is they, so this is the third person plural. Volere assimilates to the stem vorr-, then takes -anno: vorranno. Two doublings to keep right: the r of the stem (not vorano) and the n of the loro ending (not vorrano).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other double_r items.

prompt: Completa: 'Domani ____ a casa tutto il giorno.' (essere, io)
explanation: 'Tomorrow I'll be at home all day': Domani sarò a casa tutto il giorno. Essere takes a suppletive future stem sar-, one you cannot predict from the infinitive, then the regular endings: sarò. Building it from the infinitive (esserò) is the natural but wrong move. The endings themselves are the ordinary ones, so once you know sar-, the rest is regular. The accent on -ò is obligatory, and sarò is also the essere-auxiliary for the futuro anteriore.
examiner_note: Suppressed: the breadcrumb names essere and effectively the form. Suppletive stem is the whole skill.

explanation: 'She'll be happy to see you': Lei sarà felice di vederti. Lei is the subject, so this is the third person singular. Essere's suppletive stem sar- takes the ending -à: sarà. The accent matters: sarà (she will be) with the accent, never the bare sara. Don't reach back to the infinitive (esserà).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other essere items. A dropped accent (sara) is left to the marker's soft accent guard (full credit plus an orthography.accent miss), not must_not_include: sarà has no unaccented real-word collision, so per the accent-as-morpheme thread the load-bearing flag is not needed here.

prompt: Completa: 'Dopo la partita i bambini ____ stanchi.' (essere)
explanation: 'After the match the children will be tired': Dopo la partita i bambini saranno stanchi. I bambini is they, so this is the third person plural. Essere's suppletive stem sar- takes the loro ending -anno: saranno. The single-stem sar- plus the double-n ending gives the form; building from the infinitive (esseranno) or writing a single n (sarano) are the two slips.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other essere items.

explanation: 'You'll always be welcome here': Voi sarete sempre i benvenuti qui. Voi is supplied, so this is the second person plural. Essere's suppletive stem sar- takes the voi ending -ete: sarete. No accent on this person; just the suppletive stem to remember, not the infinitive (esserete).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other essere items.

prompt: Completa: 'Domani ____ i compiti tutto il pomeriggio.' (fare, io)
explanation: 'Tomorrow I'll do my homework all afternoon': Domani farò i compiti tutto il pomeriggio. Fare belongs to the small group, with dare and stare, that resists the usual -are a-to-e shift and keeps -ar- in the future. So fare gives the stem far-, then -ò: farò. Apply the shift you would use on parlare (ferò) and you have over-generalised the rule. Note the future is built on the short fa- root, not the fac- stem of faccio. The accent on -ò is obligatory.
examiner_note: Suppressed: the breadcrumb names the 'keep -ar-' exception. This is the deliberate counter-example to the regular_are a-to-e shift; ferò is the diagnostic miss.

prompt: Completa: 'Lei ti ____ una mano con il trasloco.' (dare)
explanation: 'She'll give you a hand with the move': Lei ti darà una mano con il trasloco. Lei is the subject, so this is the third person singular. Dare keeps -ar- against the usual -are shift: dar- plus -à gives darà. Apply the shift (derà) and you have treated dare like an ordinary -are verb, which here it is not. The accent on -à is obligatory.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the fare item.

prompt: Completa: 'Questo weekend ____ a casa a riposare.' (stare, noi)
explanation: 'This weekend we'll stay at home and rest': Questo weekend staremo a casa a riposare. Stare keeps -ar- in the future, against the usual -are a-to-e shift: star- plus the noi ending -emo gives staremo. Shift the vowel (steremo) and you have over-applied the regular rule.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other dare_stare_fare items.

prompt: Completa: 'L'estate prossima i miei amici ____ un lungo viaggio.' (fare)
explanation: 'Next summer my friends will take a long trip': L'estate prossima i miei amici faranno un lungo viaggio. I miei amici is they, so this is the third person plural. Fare keeps -ar- and adds the loro ending -anno: faranno. Two things to keep right: the -ar- (not feranno) and the double n (not farano).
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other dare_stare_fare items.

explanation: 'You'll see, you'll feel better soon': Vedrai che presto starai meglio. Stare keeps -ar- and takes the tu ending -ai: starai. The temptation, as with all three of dare, stare and fare, is to shift the a to e (sterai) the way every regular -are verb does; these three do not.
examiner_note: Suppressed for the same reason as the other dare_stare_fare items. Cued person kept (tu) because the subject is pro-dropped after 'Vedrai che'.

prompt: Completa: 'Quando arriverai, io ____ tutto.' (finire, azione completata prima del tuo arrivo)
explanation: 'By the time you arrive, I'll have finished everything': Quando arriverai, io avrò finito tutto. Something that will already be over by a future moment calls for the futuro anteriore, the compound future. You build it from the future of the auxiliary plus the past participle: finire takes avere, so the future of avere (avrò) plus finito gives avrò finito. The simple future (finirò) would say 'I will finish', not 'I will have finished'.
examiner_note: One combined slot per criterion 9. Auxiliary choice (avere) and participle form are PP-tree skills folded into this compound; the primary skill is producing the future auxiliary avrò. finirò is the wrong-tense miss; ho finito is present perfect.

prompt: Completa: 'Quando arriverai, Maria ____ via.' (andare, azione completata prima del tuo arrivo)
explanation: 'By the time you arrive, Maria will have left': Quando arriverai, Maria sarà andata via. The futuro anteriore of andare uses essere, because andare is a verb of motion: the future of essere (sarà) plus the participle andata gives sarà andata. With an essere auxiliary the participle agrees with the subject, and Maria is feminine singular, so it is andata, not andato. Using avere (avrà) would be the wrong auxiliary for a motion verb.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8: the future-auxiliary formation (this tree) and the participle agreement (cross-referenced to the PP tree). The full right answer is 'sarà andata'.

prompt: Completa: 'Quando tornerete, i bambini ____.' (partire, azione completata prima del vostro ritorno)
explanation: 'By the time you get back, the children will have left': Quando tornerete, i bambini saranno partiti. The futuro anteriore of partire uses essere: the future of essere (saranno) plus the participle gives the compound. With essere the participle agrees with the subject, and i bambini is masculine plural, so it is partiti. The full form is saranno partiti.
examiner_note: Two markpoints: future-auxiliary formation (this tree) and participle agreement masculine plural (PP tree).

prompt: Completa: 'Quando inizia il film, noi ____.' (mangiare, azione completata prima dell'inizio)
explanation: 'By the time the film starts, we'll have eaten': Quando inizia il film, noi avremo mangiato. The futuro anteriore of mangiare uses avere: the future of avere (avremo) plus the participle mangiato gives avremo mangiato. With an avere auxiliary and no preceding object pronoun, the participle does not agree with the subject, so it stays mangiato even though noi is plural (not mangiati). The simple future mangeremo would say 'we will eat', not 'we will have eaten'.
examiner_note: Single combined slot. The diagnostic extras in must_not_include: mangiati (wrongly agreeing an avere participle with the plural subject), saremo mangiato (wrong auxiliary).

prompt: Completa: 'Per quando ci sveglieremo, le ragazze ____.' (arrivare, azione completata prima del risveglio)
explanation: 'By the time we wake up, the girls will have arrived': Per quando ci sveglieremo, le ragazze saranno arrivate. The futuro anteriore of arrivare uses essere: the future of essere (saranno) plus the participle gives the compound. With essere the participle agrees with the subject, and le ragazze is feminine plural, so it is arrivate. The full form is saranno arrivate.
examiner_note: Two markpoints: future-auxiliary formation (this tree) and participle agreement feminine plural (PP tree). Completes the agreement set (fem sg, masc pl, fem pl) across the futuro anteriore items.
